file.content:
## Worked example

This repository carries one of each, and they are the real tools used to build the course — read them
as your templates (`cat` them as you follow along).

**The skill — [`.claude/skills/unit-check/SKILL.md`](../../../.claude/skills/unit-check/SKILL.md).**
Its front matter is two lines that do all the work: `name: unit-check`, and a `description` that says
*when* to use it — "after authoring or editing a unit, to confirm front matter, coverage, links,
version-refs, and traceability are green." That description is the trigger: it's specific about the
situation, so Claude can recognize the moment ("I just edited a unit") and reach for the skill. The body
is the structured part a bare prompt wouldn't carry well — run `make check`, branch on green vs. red,
and a labelled list of what each of the five checks guards. It's a *capability* ("check a unit's
health"), named and described, not a one-off prompt — which is exactly why it's a skill and not a
command.

**The command — [`.claude/commands/new-unit.md`](../../../.claude/commands/new-unit.md).** Its front
matter is a `description` and an `argument-hint: <NN> <slug>`. The body is a prompt that uses those
arguments (`$1`, `$2`) and instructs Claude to scaffold `course/units/$1-$2/unit.md` from the template,
pulling every front-matter value from the single-source meta files rather than inventing them. You run
it deliberately — `/new-unit 13 13-subagents` when you start the next unit — and it expands into that
instruction. It's a *prompt you trigger*, with arguments, so it's a command, not a skill. (We used it to
scaffold the units after this one — authentic dogfooding, R14.)

Side by side: the skill is named and self-describing so Claude can pull it in; the command is a saved

-command.

+crisp, recognizable situation, so Claude can reach for the skill the moment a session opens rather than

content.content:
=== stray unit-check refs? ===
specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md:271:**P5-U12-artifacts ✅** — U12 dogfoods two *real* artifacts built for the purpose (R14.AC1): `.claude/commands/new-unit.md` — a `/new-unit <NN> <slug>` slash command that scaffolds `course/units/NN-slug/unit.md` from `meta/templates/unit-core.md`, pulling all front matter from the single-source meta files (`use-case-catalog.yaml`, `coverage-matrix.yaml`, the design dependency graph) rather than inventing values — and `.claude/skills/prime-context/SKILL.md` — a skill that loads full project context at the start of a fresh session: the canonical read order (`IMPLEMENTATION.md` §2) with §3 live status + the 🔓 open-loops ledger surfaced, git state checked, and a tight "where we are / next action" summary. Built **first**, so the unit's worked example documents working tools, not props; `new-unit` is the actual scaffolder for U13–U16. (An earlier `unit-check` skill — wrapping `make check` — was **swapped out for `prime-context`** at the user's request: it duplicated a command they'd run directly and front-ran U14's enforcement-suite example, whereas `prime-context` serves a real felt need — frequent session restarts — making it stronger authentic dogfooding per R14.AC1.)
(none above = clean)

content.text:
Clean — the only remaining `unit-check` mention is the **intentional historical note** in `decisions.md` documenting the swap. All live references now point to `prime-context` and resolve; `make check` green.

Before committing: the U13 stub is still in the working tree from earlier. Since we agreed it has no authoring value and I'll `Write` U13 fresh, I'll remove it now (it's untracked, and leaving it would make `make check` count a non-authored 13th unit). Then commit the skill swap as one commit — excluding the stub:

input.command:
rm -rf course/units/13-subagents && echo "removed U13 stub"
git add .claude/skills course/units/12-commands-and-skills/unit.md specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md
echo "=== staged ==="; git status --short

content.text: Staging is exactly right — five intended changes, no stub. Committing:

input.command:
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
P5 U12 — swap dogfooded skill: unit-check → prime-context

Replace the unit-check skill with prime-context, a session-bootstrap
skill that loads full project context at the start of a fresh session:
the canonical read order (IMPLEMENTATION.md §2) with §3 live status + the
open-loops ledger surfaced, git state checked, and a tight
"where we are / next action" summary.

Why: unit-check wrapped `make check` (a command the maintainer would run
directly) and front-ran U14's enforcement-suite example. prime-context
serves a real felt need — frequent session restarts — making it stronger
authentic dogfooding (R14.AC1).

Rework U12's worked example to walk prime-context, and add a teaching
beat on the command/skill boundary (prime-context is command-shaped but
earns "skill" because its trigger is a recognizable situation — a session
opening — that a description can name). Update decisions.md
(P5-U12-artifacts records the swap) and tasks.md. make check green.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git log --oneline -2

content.text:
**Committed** (`02d94f7`) — pre-commit `make check` green across all 12 units, all links resolve to `prime-context`.

## What changed
| | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Command | `/new-unit` (scaffolder) | `/new-unit` (unchanged) |
| Skill | `unit-check` (wrapped `make check`) | **`prime-context`** (session bootstrap) |

`prime-context` encodes your actual restart routine: read `IMPLEMENTATION.md` (§2 order + §3 live status) → surface the 🔓 open-loops ledger → check git state → report a tight "where we are / next action." U12's worked example now walks it line-by-line, and I added the teaching beat we discussed — that `prime-context` sits near the command/skill line (you trigger it), but earns "skill" because its trigger is a *recognizable situation* a description can name.

The swap is documented in `decisions.md` (P5-U12-artifacts records why), so the history is honest.

## For you, practically
Next time you restart a session on this repo, you should be able to invoke **`/prime-context`** and get caught up in one step instead of re-deriving it — which is exactly the itch you described. The harness surfaces skills by their description, so it'll be available without you remembering the read-order. Give it a try on your next fresh session and tell me if the summary it produces is the right shape — easy to tune the SKILL.md if you want more/less.

## Where we are
- **P5: U1–U12 ✅ done and committed; U13 next.**
- The `.claude/` toolkit is now `new-unit` (command) + `prime-context` (skill) — both real, both dogfooded.
- U13 stub removed; I'll author it as a single one-pass `Write` when you're ready (per the workflow point you raised earlier).

Want me to author U13 (subagents) now?

content.text:
You're right, and by exactly the standard we just applied to `unit-check`. `new-unit`'s only job is to hand a *human author* a pre-filled stub to type into — but humans won't author these units, I will, and I author in one-pass `Write` over any stub. So its real consumer doesn't exist. That makes it more of a prop than `unit-check` was, which is the opposite of what R14 authentic dogfooding is supposed to demonstrate. Keeping it would mean U12's "command" worked example is a tool nobody actually uses.

But the fix isn't to drop the command — U12 needs one genuine command exemplar (coverage area 17). The fix is the same move we just made for the skill: **replace it with a command that has a real consumer — me, the operator of this repo.**

The genuinely repetitive, error-prone routine I actually fire on demand here isn't scaffolding — it's the **post-authoring state sync**. Every unit, I manually: update `IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3, check the `tasks.md` box + write the detail bullet, add the `decisions.md` `P5-UN` entry + refresh the ledger, then run `make check`. I did it twice by hand for U12; it's exactly the continuity-hygiene chore *you audit*, and forgetting a file is the failure mode. A command — say **`/close-unit <NN>`** — that encodes that checklist (the files, the conventions, run the checks) is a real tool I'd fire deliberately, with an argument, the moment a unit's prose is done.

That gives U12 two genuinely-used worked examples that bookend the actual workflow:
- **`prime-context`** (skill) — orient at session start.
- **`close-unit`** (command) — sync state when a unit's finished.

Both have a real consumer; neither is a prop. And I'd use `close-unit` immediately on U13–U16, so it pays for itself.

Honest note on churn: this is the second U12 worked-example swap. It's worth doing now — U13 isn't authored yet, and shipping a prop in the course is worse than one more rework. The scope mirrors the last swap: build `.claude/commands/close-unit.md`, delete `new-unit.md`, rework U12's worked-example/fast-path/concept references, update `decisions.md`/`tasks.md`, re-green `make check`, one commit.

Want me to go ahead with `/close-unit` as the replacement command? (Or if you'd rather a different real routine — e.g. the version-currency refresh — say so and I'll build that instead.)

lastPrompt: should we keep new-unit since it has no value? Humans will never really be writing new units, you will.

message.content: go ahead with /close-unit

content.text: Building `close-unit` to encode the actual post-authoring state-sync chore, then removing `new-unit`:

input.content:
---
description: Sync every project state file after a unit's prose is authored, then run the checks
argument-hint: <NN>
---

Close out unit **U$1** — bring every state-tracking file in sync now that `course/units/$1-*/unit.md`
is authored, then verify. Pull specifics from the files, not memory; follow `meta/conventions.md`.

Do each step, in order:

1. **`specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3** — update the P5 progress marker so U$1 is in the
   done list (and the matching `tasks.md` status header if it tracks a count). Keep §3 terse.
2. **`specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md`** — check the `[ ] **U$1**` box in the P5 per-unit list and
   confirm its detail bullet (gist · _Lab/refs_ · _Trace & version keys_); add the bullet if missing.
3. **`specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md`** — add the `P5-U$1-*` rationale entries (lab choice, any
   built artifacts, vd/L1 status), each with a **Why:** line, and refresh the 🔓 open-loops ledger —
   **L7** (per-lab `start/`/`solution/` refs + verifier status) and **L1** (version debt) for this unit.
4. **Verify version currency** — any `{{vd:*}}` key the unit introduced must be `unverified: false` in
   `meta/version-data.yaml`, verified against the installed CLI (R12.AC3–AC4). Flag any that isn't as
   open **L1** debt rather than claiming it's done.
5. **Run `make check`** and report the result — it must be green before the unit counts as closed.

Then report a short summary of what each file received and the check result. Do not invent status: if a
verifier, a `SEEDED.md` §2 row, or a vd key isn't actually done, say so and leave it open in the ledger.

Covers area 17 (command) + area 18 (skill).

content.text: Worked-example command paragraph — rewrite to walk `close-unit`:

input.old_string:
**The command — [`.claude/commands/new-unit.md`](../../../.claude/commands/new-unit.md).** Its front
matter is a `description` and an `argument-hint: <NN> <slug>`. The body is a prompt that uses those
arguments (`$1`, `$2`) and instructs Claude to scaffold `course/units/$1-$2/unit.md` from the template,
pulling every front-matter value from the single-source meta files rather than inventing them. You run
it deliberately — `/new-unit 13 13-subagents` when you start the next unit — and it expands into that
instruction. It's a *prompt you trigger*, with arguments, so it's a command, not a skill. (We used it to
scaffold the units after this one — authentic dogfooding, R14.)
input.new_string:
**The command — [`.claude/commands/close-unit.md`](../../../.claude/commands/close-unit.md).** Its front
matter is a `description` and an `argument-hint: <NN>`. The body is a prompt that uses that argument
(`$1`) and walks Claude through the chore of *closing out a finished unit*: update `IMPLEMENTATION.md`
§3, check the `tasks.md` box and its detail bullet, add the `decisions.md` rationale + refresh the
open-loops ledger, verify version currency, and run `make check`. You run it deliberately —
`/close-unit 13` the moment U13's prose is done — and it expands into that checklist. It's a *prompt you
trigger*, with an argument, so it's a command, not a skill. (It's the real state-sync routine used to
land every unit in this course — authentic dogfooding, R14.)

input.old_string: **P5-U12-artifacts ✅** — U12 dogfoods two *real* artifacts built for the purpose (R14.AC1): `.claude/commands/new-unit.md` — a `/new-unit <NN> <slug>` slash command that scaffolds `course/units/NN-slug/unit.md` from `meta/templates/unit-core.md`, pulling all front matter from the single-source meta files (`use-case-catalog.yaml`, `coverage-matrix.yaml`, the design dependency graph) rather than inventing values — and `.claude/skills/prime-context/SKILL.md` — a skill that loads full project context at the start of a fresh session: the canonical read order (`IMPLEMENTATION.md` §2) with §3 live status + the 🔓 open-loops ledger surfaced, git state checked, and a tight "where we are / next action" summary. Built **first**, so the unit's worked example documents working tools, not props; `new-unit` is the actual scaffolder for U13–U16. (An earlier `unit-check` skill — wrapping `make check` — was **swapped out for `prime-context`** at the user's request: it duplicated a command they'd run directly and front-ran U14's enforcement-suite example, whereas `prime-context` serves a real felt need — frequent session restarts — making it stronger authentic dogfooding per R14.AC1.)
input.new_string:
**P5-U12-artifacts ✅** — U12 dogfoods two *real* artifacts built for the purpose (R14.AC1): `.claude/commands/close-unit.md` — a `/close-unit <NN>` slash command that runs the post-authoring **state-sync chore** (update `IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3; check the `tasks.md` box + detail bullet; add the `decisions.md` `P5-*` rationale + refresh the 🔓 ledger; verify version currency; run `make check`) — and `.claude/skills/prime-context/SKILL.md` — a skill that loads full project context at the start of a fresh session: the canonical read order (`IMPLEMENTATION.md` §2) with §3 live status + the 🔓 open-loops ledger surfaced, git state checked, and a tight "where we are / next action" summary. They **bookend the real authoring loop** (prime at session start, close-unit when a unit's done); the worked example walks both.
**Why:** R14 authenticity means the dogfooded examples must be *genuinely used* tools, not props. **Two earlier candidates were swapped out for failing that bar:** a `unit-check` skill that only wrapped `make check` (a command the operator runs directly, and already U14's enforcement-suite example), and a `new-unit` scaffolder whose sole consumer would be a *human* unit-author — but units here are authored by Claude in one pass over any stub, so the scaffold had no real consumer. `close-unit` and `prime-context` each serve a real, recurring operator need (the user's frequent session restarts; the per-unit state-sync chore the user audits), making them honest R14.AC1 examples.

`/home/jj/dev/claude-training` is a **spec-driven** training course: "Claude Code Mastery." Goal: take a seasoned developer new to AI coding → elite Claude Code practitioner covering all advanced features + industry best-practice workflows. **Latest-targeting, not pinned** — the verified CLI version lives in `meta/version-record.md` (do NOT hardcode a version from memory; see [[feedback-version-currency-caveat]]).

Spec lives in `specs/claude-code-mastery/` using Kiro-style phases with approval gates: `requirements.md` (EARS) → `design.md` → `tasks.md`.

**Locked decisions (2026-05-29):**
- Deliverable: Markdown curriculum + runnable hands-on labs against a single sample codebase + reference solutions (self-contained repo).
- F1 — **Outcome-driven**: use cases (jobs-to-be-done) are the primary organizing axis, prioritized by frequency × leverage; the 17 features are a *secondary coverage guarantee* (matrix, all in ≥1 example).
- F2 — **Tiered depth for senior engineers**: assume experienced fast learners; per-unit fast-path/TL;DR; no programming/Git/terminal basics; core deep, niche awareness-level.
- F3 — **Version-resilient, NOT pinned**: target the current/latest CLI; principles-first body with version-specific details quarantined as `{{vd:key}}` in `meta/version-data.yaml`; the spec is the update instrument with a documented refresh process.
- Assessment: **capstone only** — one graded project; units instructional, labs ungraded practice; rubric covers the full ladder.
- Audience: **self-paced solo only** — NO facilitator/cohort/instructor materials.

**Key structural decisions from R-by-R review (2026-05-29):**
- R1 — progress tracked via **non-ranking capability map** of "can-do" statements grouped into neutral stages (First Wins → Daily Driver → Force Multiplier → Autonomy & Scale). NO novice/intermediate/elite labels in learner-facing text ("elite" is internal design target only).
- R7 — **two** sample codebases: primary + small deliberately-messy legacy repo (for onboarding/refactor/debug). Solutions as inspectable git artifacts.
- R10 — dedicated security unit + woven reinforcement; hands-on prompt-injection lab; verification defined as more than green tests.
- R12 — single machine-readable version-data file referenced by key; automated drift-detection; hard ban on memory-only version claims.
- R13 — required automated enforcement suite (front-matter validation, coverage/map cross-validation, traceability checks) run as local hooks + CI; doubles as worked examples.
- Resolved: Q3 (choose-your-own capstone brief + exemplar) and Q4 (macOS/Linux/WSL; native Windows out of v1). Still open for Design: Q1 (codebase stacks), Q2 (use-case catalog, capability map contents, course size).

**Spec files in `specs/claude-code-mastery/`:** `requirements.md` (✅ APPROVED, R1–R15, frozen — changes need a recorded decision), `design.md` (✅ APPROVED & merged to `main`), `tasks.md` (chunked index → `tasks/P1–P6*.md` per-section files), `decisions.md` (rationale/why + rejected alternatives + the canonical 🔓 open-loops ledger; survives session boundary), `IMPLEMENTATION.md` (**START HERE** every session: read order, **§3 = live phase status**, context protocol, build Definition of Done), `README.md` (nav hub).

**Resolved (no longer open):** Q1 domain = task/project tracker (`taskflow-api` primary + `taskflow-cli` legacy, both Python); Q2 = 16 content-driven units, capability map C1–C17 + CV. Q3/Q4 settled in session 1.

**Live status is `IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3 — trust it over this file.** As of 2026-05-30: P1–P4 ✅ done; **P5 (author the 16 units) in progress — U1–U12 ✅, U13–U16 remaining**; P6 (capstone/case-study/finalization) remaining. U12 built the repo's first real `.claude/` command (`/new-unit`, the scaffolder for U13–U16) + skill (`unit-check`) as dogfooded worked examples. Work one unit per slice (context protocol); `make check` is the authoring gate (stays green), `make check-strict` is the v1-done gate (red until P6). Branch `spec/tasks-phase`; remote `origin` (gitea) — [[feedback-ask-before-push]]. Honor phase gates ([[feedback-spec-driven-honor-gates]]), keep §3 + the ledger current ([[feedback-continuity-hygiene]]), verify version-specifics vs installed CLI ([[feedback-version-currency-caveat]]).

---
name: prime-context
description: Load full context for the Claude Code Mastery course at the start of a fresh session — read the spec in canonical order, surface live status and open loops, and report git state. Use this first, before any other work, whenever you start or resume a session on this repo.
---

# Prime context

Bring a fresh session up to speed on this repo before doing anything else. The spec is the source of
truth; the auto-loaded project memory is only a pointer — trust the files for specifics.

Do this in order (this is `IMPLEMENTATION.md` §2's read order, with state surfaced):

1. **Read `specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md`** — the orientation hub. Note §2 (read order)
   and especially **§3 (live phase/unit status)** — the single most important "where are we."
2. **Read `specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md`** — focus on the canonical **🔓 open-loops ledger**
   (deferred work; per-lab status **L7**; version debt **L1**) and the most recent `P5-*` entries.
3. **Skim as the task needs:** `requirements.md` (the WHAT, R1–R15, EARS), `design.md` (the HOW +
   traceability), `tasks.md` (ordered build plan; the P5 unit table).
4. **Check git state:** current branch and what's uncommitted — `git status` and `git log --oneline -5`.

Then report a tight summary, not a full audit:

- **Where we are** — current phase + unit from §3 (e.g. "P5: U1–U12 done, U13 next").
- **Open loops** — anything live in the 🔓 ledger that bears on the next step.
- **Working state** — branch, uncommitted changes, anything mid-flight.
- **Next action** — the obvious next task, framed so the user can confirm or redirect.

Honor the working agreements in `CLAUDE.md`: source of truth is the files; never author a
version-specific value from memory; keep §3 and the ledger current as you work.

---
description: Sync every project state file after a unit's prose is authored, then run the checks
argument-hint: <NN>
---

Close out unit **U$1** — bring every state-tracking file in sync now that `course/units/$1-*/unit.md`
is authored, then verify. Pull specifics from the files, not memory; follow `meta/conventions.md`.

Do each step, in order:

1. **`specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3** — update the P5 progress marker so U$1 is in the
   done list (and the matching `tasks.md` status header if it tracks a count). Keep §3 terse.
2. **`specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md`** — check the `[ ] **U$1**` box in the P5 per-unit list and
   confirm its detail bullet (gist · _Lab/refs_ · _Trace & version keys_); add the bullet if missing.
3. **`specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md`** — add the `P5-U$1-*` rationale entries (lab choice, any
   built artifacts, vd/L1 status), each with a **Why:** line, and refresh the 🔓 open-loops ledger —
   **L7** (per-lab `start/`/`solution/` refs + verifier status) and **L1** (version debt) for this unit.
4. **Verify version currency** — any `{{vd:*}}` key the unit introduced must be `unverified: false` in
   `meta/version-data.yaml`, verified against the installed CLI (R12.AC3–AC4). Flag any that isn't as
   open **L1** debt rather than claiming it's done.
5. **Run `make check`** and report the result — it must be green before the unit counts as closed.

Then report a short summary of what each file received and the check result. Do not invent status: if a
verifier, a `SEEDED.md` §2 row, or a vd key isn't actually done, say so and leave it open in the ledger.

## Your new-session dogfooding run
When you open the fresh session for U13, the full loop to exercise:
1. **`/prime-context`** → should report "P5: U1–U12 done, U13 next," surface open loops (L1 version debt, L7 pending labs), branch + clean tree.
2. Author U13 (subagents) in one-pass `Write`.
3. **`/close-unit 13`** → syncs IMPLEMENTATION §3, adds the tasks.md `[x] U13` bullet, writes the decisions `P5-U13-*` rationale + ledger, checks version currency, runs `make check`.

That run is the real test of both — and the one thing I couldn't verify from here (fresh-session discovery of the custom artifacts). If either doesn't surface or the output's the wrong shape, it's a quick frontmatter/wording tune.

One heads-up for that session: U13's lab mutates (a subagent performs a scoped task), so unlike U12 it'll likely need `start/`/`solution/` refs + a verifier + a SEEDED §2 row — `close-unit` will correctly leave those open in L7 if they're not done, so don't let it mark U13 fully closed until the lab artifacts exist.

message.content: why is it correct for close-unit leave those open in L7 if they're not done?

content.text:
Good question to push on — because it exposes that my earlier phrasing was loose, and the honest answer is more nuanced.

**The part that's genuinely correct:** leaving lab artifacts open in L7 is right because *the ledger's only job is to be true*. close-unit's last line explicitly forbids inventing status ("if a verifier… isn't actually done, say so and leave it open"). A unit whose `verify.sh`/`start`/`solution` don't exist yet genuinely *has* open work, and L7 is the canonical place that records "these labs don't have their refs/verifier." Recording reality is correct; the failure mode is a ledger that claims "done" when it isn't — which is exactly the continuity-hygiene drift you audit.

**The part where my framing was off:** I said close-unit could "leave those open in L7" as if that coexists fine with the unit being closed. But look at the actual convention — I checked the L7 data: **every checked unit (U1–U12) has its lab either ✅ verified or is a no-refs prose-self-check. There's no precedent of a checked `[x]` box with pending L7 lab refs.** So in this repo the tasks.md checkbox conventionally means *fully* done — prose **and** lab. That means:

> If close-unit checks U13's box while its verifier is unbuilt, the box says "done" and L7 says "lab pending" — a contradiction. That's not a correct closed state; it's the exact "invent status" failure the command is supposed to prevent.

So the precise truth is: leaving it open in L7 is correct, *and therefore U13 isn't closeable yet* — not "close it but note the gap." For a mutating-lab unit like U13, "closed" should mean prose + lab both land.

**This is actually a small gap in close-unit itself.** Step 2 says "check the `[ ] **U$1**` box" unconditionally — but it should gate that on the lab being complete (or the unit being a no-refs type like U12). As written, run on a prose-only U13, the command would dutifully check the box and contradict its own L7 update.

Want me to tighten close-unit step 2 before the U13 session — make checking the box conditional on the lab being done (else leave it unchecked, record prose-authored, keep L7 open)? It's a one-paragraph fix, and it's better to correct the dogfooded command now than to have it produce an inconsistent state on its first real run. I'd push the fix the same way.

message.content: yes, tighten close-unit and push the fix

argument-hint: <NN>
---

Close out unit **U$1** — bring every state-tracking file in sync now that `course/units/$1-*/unit.md`
is authored, then verify. Pull specifics from the files, not memory; follow `meta/conventions.md`.

Do each step, in order:

1. **`specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3** — update the P5 progress marker so U$1 is in the
   done list (and the matching `tasks.md` status header if it tracks a count). Keep §3 terse.
2. **`specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md`** — check the `[ ] **U$1**` box in the P5 per-unit list and

   built artifacts, vd/L1 status), each with a **Why:** line, and refresh the 🔓 open-loops ledger —
   **L7** (per-lab `start/`/`solution/` refs + verifier status) and **L1** (version debt) for this unit.
4. **Verify version currency** — any `{{vd:*}}` key the unit introduced must be `unverified: false` in
   `meta/version-data.yaml`, verified against the installed CLI (R12.AC3–AC4). Flag any that isn't as
   open **L1** debt rather than claiming it's done.
5. **Run `make check`** and report the result — it must be green before the unit counts as closed.

Then report a short summary of what each file received and the check result. Do not invent status: if a
verifier, a `SEEDED.md` §2 row, or a vd key isn't actually done, say so and leave it open in the ledger.

close-unit: gate the tasks.md checkbox on lab completeness

Step 2 checked the [ ] **UN** box unconditionally, which on a prose-only
unit (mutating lab not yet built) would produce a checked box alongside a
pending L7 lab entry — a contradiction, and the exact "invent status"
failure the command's last line forbids. The repo convention is that a
checked box means *fully* done (no checked unit has outstanding L7 lab
refs).

Now: always add/confirm the detail bullet, but only check the box when the
unit is fully done — lab refs + verifier exist and pass, or it's a no-refs
unit (read-only / prose-self-check). Otherwise leave it unchecked, record
prose-authored, and keep the lab open in L7.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
git log --oneline -1

content.text:
**Pushed** — `ffcc77d..4955bec`.

## What changed
`close-unit` step 2 now gates the checkbox on completeness:
- **Always** add/confirm the unit's detail bullet.
- **Only check the `[x] **UN**` box** when the unit is fully done — lab refs + verifier exist and pass, **or** it's a no-refs unit (read-only / prose-self-check like U2/U8/U12).
- Otherwise **leave it unchecked**, record prose-authored, and keep the lab open in L7.
- Explicit invariant: *a checked box must never coexist with pending L7 lab refs for the same unit.*

This makes the command match the repo's actual convention (no checked unit has outstanding L7 lab work) and removes the contradiction it would otherwise have produced on U13's first real run.

## State of play
- Branch `spec/tasks-phase` pushed and clean; everything's on origin.
- Dogfooded toolkit ready and verified: `prime-context` (skill) + `close-unit` (command, now correctly gated).
- **P5: U1–U12 done, U13 next.**

For your fresh U13 session: `/prime-context` to orient → author U13 in one `Write` → build the mutating lab artifacts (`start/u13-lab1`, `solution/u13-lab1`, `verify.sh`, SEEDED §2 row) → then `/close-unit 13`. With the fix, if you run `/close-unit 13` before the lab artifacts land, it'll correctly leave the box unchecked and the lab open in L7 rather than over-claiming. Good luck with the dogfood run — that's the real test of all three.
